mappings.rationaleAI: Al-Ghazali's critique of philosophy, defence of Sunni theology, and embrace of Sufism directly address the Middle Ages of Islam (A.5), Islamic theology and philosophy (B.4), and the mystical path of Sufism (B.5).
The Arab conquests spreading Islam from Arabia across the Middle East, North Africa, and into Iberia directly address the foundations and early expansion of Islam (A.3).

The Abbasid caliphate's golden age from 750 to the mid-tenth century, including patronage of Arabic philosophy, mathematics, and medicine, systematic translation of Greek texts, and integration of Persian, Arab, and Turkic traditions shaped Islamic intellectual and political foundations.

Directly examines the origins and development of Sharia and fiqh, covering the four principal sources of Islamic law (Quran, Hadith, ijma, qiyas), the emergence of classical legal schools (madhabs), and the role of ulama in systematizing jurisprudence during the early Abbasid period.
The Translation Movement of Greek texts into Arabic during the Abbasid Caliphate directly addresses the development of Islamic intellectual culture (A.4) and philosophical trends in Islam (B.4).
The Safavid Dynasty establishing Shia Islam as state religion with its Sufi origins directly addresses the Middle Ages of Islam (A.5) and Shi'ism (C.2.d).

The Arabian Nights as a collection of Middle Eastern folk tales spanning Arabic, Persian, and Indian sources is relevant to mythical elements of Islamic beliefs (B.6) and Islamic art and cultural expression (C.4).
Antarah ibn Shaddad as a pre-Islamic Arabian poet illuminates the Jahiliyyah literary tradition and pre-Islamic Arabian society — directly relevant to the pre-Islamic setting in Arabia (A.1).
Zoroastrianism's influence on Islam including concepts of heaven, hell, angels, and resurrection is relevant to the pre-Islamic religious background (A.1).
Angels in Islamic tradition including Jibril's role in Quranic revelation directly addresses doctrines concerning intermediate beings in Islam (C.1.d).
Muhammad as the Seal of the Prophets directly addresses doctrines concerning Muhammad and the nature of prophecy (C.1.e).
Islamic Jannah (paradise) as a conception of the afterlife directly addresses Islamic eschatological doctrines (C.1.f).

Explicitly examines Islamic fundamentalist movements in the twentieth century alongside their violent manifestations, addressing why fundamentalism has flourished despite secularization and its relationship to Islam in the modern world.
The Volga Vikings' trade with the Abbasid Caliphate and Ibn Fadlan's account illustrate the early expansion and cultural interactions of Islam (A.3).

The 12th-century Renaissance covers the recovery of classical texts through Arabic translations and contact with Islamic civilisation — relevant to Islamic intellectual development (A.4) and philosophy (B.4).
Alchemy's transmission through Islamic civilization with figures like Jabir ibn Hayyan is relevant to Islamic philosophical and scientific traditions (B.4).

Examines the foundational Sunni-Shia division originating in the seventh-century succession crisis after Muhammad's death, the massacre at Karbala as a defining moment of Shia identity, and how a political dispute over legitimate authority deepened into distinct legal schools, ritual practices, and communal identities.

Al-Kindi was the first major Islamic philosopher to argue that philosophy and Islam were compatible, supervised Arabic translations of Aristotle, and authored over 250 works — a foundational figure in Islamic philosophical trends during the early Abbasid era.

Rumi's Masnavi and Divan-e Shams are central works of Sufi mystical poetry exploring divine love and spiritual union, while his followers established the Mevlevi Order (Whirling Dervishes) — directly illustrating Sufism as the mystical path (B.5), Sufi orders (C.2.f), and the expression of Islamic faith through the arts (C.4.b).

The 11th-century Muslim polymath al-Biruni produced the first Muslim scholarly account of Hindu culture and corresponded with Avicenna, exemplifying cross-cultural knowledge exchange and the breadth of Islamic philosophical and scientific inquiry during the Middle Ages of Islam.

Nizami's Khamsa integrates philosophy, astronomy, and legends within Persian romantic verse, representing a major expression of Islamic faith and themes through literary art and exemplifying the cultural flowering of the medieval Islamic world.

Features an 8th-century Iraqi Koran among the earliest surviving sacred texts and examines how writing materials and techniques enabled Islam's spread, connecting the material history of Quranic manuscript production with the development of Islamic religious authority.

Maimonides was deeply shaped by Arabic-Islamic intellectual traditions including Avicenna and Al-Farabi, and influenced Islamic philosophy reciprocally, illustrating the cross-pollination between Jewish and Islamic philosophical thought within the medieval Islamic world.

Ferdowsi's epic recounts Iran's narrative through the Arab conquest and served as a vehicle for Persian linguistic preservation against Arabization, illuminating the cultural dynamics of early Islamic expansion and the interplay between Persian and Islamic literary traditions.
